steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The researchers argue 'invasive' and 'native' describe temporary circumstances because climate change will shift where animals can live. We need the choice that correctly applies this logic to Maryland's flathead catfish.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — it's useful at present for the state to distinguish between invasive and native species in some instances but not in the case of the flathead catfish.: The researchers' argument applies to all species; no exception is made for any particular one. ✗
  • Choice B — the state was previously home to some flathead catfish but they were outcompeted by invading species.: This reverses the timeline — the argument is about future climate change, not historical range. ✗
  • Choice C — the state's designation of the flathead catfish as invasive may be appropriate now but not in the future.: This is exactly the time-bound shift the researchers predict: current conditions justify the label today, but climate change will alter those conditions. ✓
  • Choice D — even if Earth's climate doesn't change in the way scientists predict, the flathead catfish will likely establish itself in the state.: This undermines the researchers' argument, which depends on climate change as the mechanism for range shifts. ✗
C is correct.
